revision.comment: 7 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:威信財 up to rev 102441388
revision.text:
'''威信財'''（いしんざい、{{lang-en|prestige goods}}）とは、権力を象徴する財物を指す[[文化人類学]]の用語{{sfn|コトバンク: 威信財}}。ただしこの用語の定義は一定ではない。日本においては「物質的な生存には必要ないが、社会関係を維持するためには不可欠の生産物」としたK.エクホルムの定義を引用することが多いが、単に「威信を示す貴重品（{{lang-en|status symbols}}）」という意味合いで使われることも少なくない{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=90}}{{sfn|辻田淳一郎|2019|p=255-258}}。 元々は[[経済人類学]]で用いられた用語で、階層化が進展した首長制社会において、上位層が贈与・交換を通じてその地位を確認し、かつ相互関係を取り結ぶ役割を負った器物を指す。また、これによって維持される社会体系を'''威信財システム'''と呼び、初期国家が成立する前段階における複雑な階層化社会を説明するのに有効な社会モデルのひとつとして提案された{{sfn|辻田淳一郎|2019|p=255-258}}{{sfn|辻田淳一郎|2019|p=258-259}}。しかし、日本の[[考古学]]界では、器物の社会的機能や流通様態の研究に踏み込まずに特定の器物に威信財のレッテルを貼るだけの濫用が行われた{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=81-86}}。 == 欧米での研究 == === 威信財論の黎明期 === 威信財論の緒言は、1922年の[[ブロニスワフ・マリノフスキ]]による[[クラ (交易)|クラ]]研究や、1897年の[[フランツ・ボアズ]]の[[ポトラッチ]]研究とされる。これらの交換や贈与の発見により、非市場的交易の究明が進んだ。1954年に発表された[[マルセル・モース]]は『[[贈与論]]』で、贈与交換の原理に「霊的な力」の概念がある事を指摘し、経済現象にとどまらない「全体的社会事実」であることを明らかにした{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=75-81}}。その後、欧米での威信財論は、器物の交換局面に焦点を当てた研究と、社会構造論・国家形成論の枠組みを構築する研究に2分化する{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=96-97}}。特に後者について、1957年に[[カール・ポランニー]]が、貨幣経済以前の古代社会において威信財など財宝を支払い手段とする「財宝財政」と、食料などを支払い手段とする「基本物資財政」の2つが存在した事を主張した説は、その後の威信財論に大きな影響を与えた。このポランニーの視点は、1960年代以降に[[進化主義#新進化主義|新進化主義人類学]]と[[構造主義的マルクス主義]]に取り入れられ、エクホルムらによって器物の流通と婚姻のコントロールにより社会関係が維持・再生産・発展するという視点が現れる{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=75-81}}。 === 威信財システム === [[File:Prestige goods system.svg|400px|thumb|威信財システムのモデル図]] 以上のような成果を踏まえて、1977年に{{仮リンク|ジョナサン・フリードマン|en|Jonathan Friedman}}とマイケル・ローランズは首長制社会から初期国家に至るプロセスを次の5つのステージに設定し、実例に基づき社会進化の説明を試みた{{sfn|辻田淳一郎|2019|p=255-258}}。 * 部族システム - 単系の出自集団（[[リネージ]]）や、共通の祖先をもつと認識する氏族（[[氏族|クラン]]）などに基づき、婚姻や交換を行う社会。 * アジア的国家 - 親族集団同士に序列化が生じ、より上位の親族集団との婚姻関係により社会における階層的位置づけが規定される社会。 * 威信財システム - 各地で首長社会が進展し、各地の上位層が交流を持つことで地域間に威信財の贈与・交換が行われ、同盟的な関係を結ぶ社会。 * 領域国家/都市国家 - 経済的な発展により特定の中心地を核として中央集権的に編成される社会（領域国家）。あるいは経済が独立的に発展する社会（都市国家）。 * 帝国 - 政治的・軍事的に統合される社会。 このうち、威信財システムは[[トンガ]]の首長制社会や中国の[[西周 (王朝)|西周]]代をモデル化したもので、首長制社会が広域に展開する際に地域間の政治的関係が取り結ばれるあり方を示すモデルとして示された{{sfn|辻田淳一郎|2019|p=255-258}}。こうした社会において各地域集団内で威信財を入手できるかは、上位層との婚姻関係にかかっており、上位層から下位層への威信財の贈与と下位層から上位層への貢ぎ物の交換によって政治的同盟関係（上下関係）が維持・再生産される{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=75-81}}。ここでの威信財は、長距離交易によって上位層にもたらされる器物とされた。また、こうした社会構造は、上位層への威信財の供給が不安定になると崩壊する可能性を内包する、流動的な性質があるとされた{{sfn|辻田淳一郎|2019|p=258-259}}。 フリードマンらの社会進化モデルは「[[後成説]]モデル」と呼ばれ、高い評価を受けたが、現在では必ずしも単一のプロセスで社会が進化するとは考えられていない。しかし、考古資料から権力資源を分析することで、世界各地の国家形成を具体的に比較検討しえる手法として評価されている{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=75-81}}。 == 日本での研究 == 1980年代まで日本の古代国家形成論は『[[記紀]]』などの文献史学が中心であったが、文献資料の乏しさや[[史料批判]]の深化により研究が下火となり、他分野との交流も少なく蛸壺化が起きていた。1990年代に[[都出比呂志]]が、考古学と[[文化人類学]]に立脚した初期国家論を提唱すると、それを画期として考古学分野での国家形成論の研究が盛んになる{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=4-7}}{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=8-17}}。そうした中で威信財論は、日本考古学界が好む[[唯物論]]的アプローチと相性が良かった事もあり注目を浴びるようになった{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=81-86}}。 === 日本における研究史 === [[File:Mirror with triangular rim and dragon and tiger design, 4 of 12, Japan, Kofun period, 200s-300s AD, bronze - Tokyo National Museum - Tokyo, Japan - DSC09171.jpg|thumb|古墳時代初期の威信財のひとつ
三角縁神獣鏡]] {{Double image aside|right|稲荷山古墳出土鉄剣 (表面).JPG|110|稲荷山古墳出土鉄剣 (裏面).JPG|110|古墳時代中期の威信財のひとつ
銘文鉄剣（[[稲荷山古墳出土鉄剣]]）}} 威信財論が本格的に日本に導入されるのは1990年代であるが、それ以前から威信財論的な視点はあった。特に1950年代に[[小林行雄]]が提唱した[[三角縁神獣鏡]]の同笵鏡論は威信財論を先取りした成果と評価されている{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=81-82}}{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=95-96}}。小林は、同笵鏡論と伝世鏡論を両輪として[[古墳]]の発生・男系世襲制の成立・[[ヤマト王権|大和政権]]の勢力拡大などの社会変化を[[銅鏡]]を中心に論じた{{sfn|辻田淳一郎|2019|p=116-118}}。この小林の仮説は実証面から批判が相次ぎ、2010年代ではほぼ否定されているが、古墳時代の開始過程の研究に今なお影響を残している{{sfn|辻田淳一郎|2019|p=118-120}}。 欧米での威信財研究を最初に日本の考古学に導入したのは1985年の穴沢咊光の論考である。しかし穴沢の発表はマイナーな媒体であったため普及しなかった{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=81-82}}。 1990年代前半からは、古墳時代を中心に多彩な器物が威信財と見なされた。[[松木武彦]]の[[鉄鏃]]の流通における「威信財重層モデル」など意欲的な提示もあったが、全体的には理論検討や明確な基準もなく、威信財に認定する傾向があった{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=83}}。 1990年代末からは、威信財の用語が濫用される。[[旧石器時代]]の[[石槍]]、[[縄文時代]]の硬玉製大珠・木の葉文浅鉢形土器、[[弥生時代]]の青銅製武器形祭器・[[銅鐸]]、[[古墳時代]]の銅鏡・金銅製品・石製品・武器武具・馬具、[[歴史時代]]の[[陶磁器]]・[[茶器]]など、あらゆる時代の貴重品や高級品が威信財と言い換えられた。その一方で、こうした器物が如何なる機能や役割を果たしたのかという重要な論点が看過された{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=83-86}}。 理論面での検討に先鞭をつけたのは[[河野一隆]]である。河野は威信財を「生産型威信財」と「非生産型威信財」に区別し、弥生時代から古墳時代の社会を考察した。特に「外部から威信財が継続して流入し、威信財が氾濫して社会システムが破綻する事態を、威信財の副葬により消費することで克服した」とする威信財の更新という視点は画期的であった{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=83-86}}{{sfn|辻田淳一郎|2019|p=259-260}}。 2000年代からは日本における威信財論の弱点を克服する動きが見られるようになる。石村智は、流通パターンから「循環する威信財」「分配する威信財」「生産される威信財」に類型化し、[[オセアニア]]諸地域と日本列島の親族関係・生態系・民族事例を検討し、「威信財システムへの依存から脱却することにより社会の階層化が行われる」とする視点を提示した{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=83-86}}{{sfn|辻田淳一郎|2019|p=259-260}}。 [[辻田淳一郎]]は、フリードマンらの威信財システムの枠組みを参考に、日本列島の国家形成プロセスのモデル構築を行った{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=83-86}}。辻田は、威信財を「入手・使用・消費が上位層に独占され、且つそのサイクルが社会的再生産のプロセスと不可分に埋め込まれたもの」と定義し{{sfn|辻田淳一郎|2019|p=258-259}}、銅鏡や[[鉄剣・鉄刀銘文|銘文鉄剣]]が中央政権と地方有力者を繋げる器物であると指摘した{{sfn|辻田淳一郎|2019|p=329-330}}{{sfn|辻田淳一郎|2019|p=333-334}}。さらに、こうした威信財システムが機能した古墳時代においても前期と中期で様相が異なる事や、後期に至ると[[屯倉|ミヤケ制]]・[[国造|国造制]]・[[部民制]]が整い威信財が役割を終えることで社会構造が変化し、古代国家が誕生したと推測した{{sfn|辻田淳一郎|2019|p=371-373}}。 === 威信財研究の問題点 === [[下垣仁志]]は、国家形成論における威信財論の可能性を認めつつも、一方で方法論的な限界や問題点がある事を指摘している{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=86-91}}。 1つ目は物質的資料の残存性である。文化人類学の研究では、威信財が繊維製品や羽毛などの有機物である例が少なくない。『[[魏志倭人伝]]』に記載された[[邪馬台国]]への下賜品も「絳地交龍錦」などの高級織物が大半を占めており、これらが威信財として機能していた可能性は高い。しかし、考古資料は残存性の高い金属器や石製品などの無機物に偏ってしまい、全ての器物の移動を復元することは困難である。また、女性や奴隷の労働力など非物質的な交換を復元することも至難である。こうした非物質的な財は、威信財の贈与に対する貢納として用いられることが多いが、実態が明らかでないため類推に頼らざるを得ず、結果として貢納・反対贈与はしばしば矮小化される{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=86-91}}。 2つ目は器物とそれを保有した人間の関係性が軽視されがちな点である。威信財論は、社会構造の発展・再生産で器物が果たした役割に焦点があてられる事が多い。そうした検証は重要であるが、その反面、あたかも器物が威信をパッキングして集団間を移動するかのように描かれる事が少なくない。しかし実際には所有者が器物の使用、あるいは保管を通して、威信を生成し価値を付与したはずである。こうした威信の生成する行為をなおざりにすると、結果として威信財の交換・移動が行われた要因を軽視することになりうる{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=86-91}}。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 出典 === {{Reflist|20em}} == 参考文献 == ;書籍 * {{Cite book|和書|author=辻田淳一郎 |title=鏡の古代史 |year=2019 |publisher=[[KADOKAWA]] |series=角川選書|isbn=978-4-04-703663-5 |ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=下垣仁志|authorlink=下垣仁志 |title=古墳時代の国家形成 |year=2018 |publisher=吉川弘文館 |isbn=978-4-642-09352-1 |ref=harv}} ;辞典など * “[[コトバンク]]”. [[朝日新聞社]], [[CARTA HOLDINGS|VOYAGE MARKETING]]. ** {{Cite web|和書|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A8%81%E4%BF%A1%E8%B2%A1-1268539 |title=威信財 |accessdate=2021-10-30|ref={{sfnref|コトバンク: 威信財}}}}（『デジタル[[大辞泉]]』ほかより転載）。 == 関連項目 == * [[非市場経済]] * [[同笵鏡論・伝世鏡論]] {{DEFAULTSORT:いしんさい}} [[Category:文化人類学]] [[Category:経済人類学]] [[Category:日本の考古学]] [[Category:先史時代 (日本)]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[威信財]] to [[Prestige goods]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
'''威信財'''（いしんざい、{{lang-en|prestige goods}}）とは、権力を象徴する財物を指す[[文化人類学]]の用語{{sfn|コトバンク: 威信財}}。ただしこの用語の定義は一定ではない。日本においては「物質的な生存には必要ないが、社会関係を維持するためには不可欠の生産物」としたK.エクホルムの定義を引用することが多いが、単に「威信を示す貴重品（{{lang-en|status symbols}}）」という意味合いで使われることも少なくない{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=90}}{{sfn|辻田淳一郎|2019|p=255-258}}。 元々は[[経済人類学]]で用いられた用語で、階層化が進展した首長制社会において、上位層が贈与・交換を通じてその地位を確認し、かつ相互関係を取り結ぶ役割を負った器物を指す。また、これによって維持される社会体系を'''威信財システム'''と呼び、初期国家が成立する前段階における複雑な階層化社会を説明するのに有効な社会モデルのひとつとして提案された{{sfn|辻田淳一郎|2019|p=255-258}}{{sfn|辻田淳一郎|2019|p=258-259}}。しかし、日本の[[考古学]]界では、器物の社会的機能や流通様態の研究に踏み込まずに特定の器物に威信財のレッテルを貼るだけの濫用が行われた{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=81-86}}。 == 欧米での研究 == === 威信財論の黎明期 === 威信財論の緒言は、1922年の[[ブロニスワフ・マリノフスキ]]による[[クラ (交易)|クラ]]研究や、1897年の[[フランツ・ボアズ]]の[[ポトラッチ]]研究とされる。これらの交換や贈与の発見により、非市場的交易の究明が進んだ。1954年に発表された[[マルセル・モース]]は『[[贈与論]]』で、贈与交換の原理に「霊的な力」の概念がある事を指摘し、経済現象にとどまらない「全体的社会事実」であることを明らかにした{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=75-81}}。その後、欧米での威信財論は、器物の交換局面に焦点を当てた研究と、社会構造論・国家形成論の枠組みを構築する研究に2分化する{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=96-97}}。特に後者について、1957年に[[カール・ポランニー]]が、貨幣経済以前の古代社会において威信財など財宝を支払い手段とする「財宝財政」と、食料などを支払い手段とする「基本物資財政」の2つが存在した事を主張した説は、その後の威信財論に大きな影響を与えた。このポランニーの視点は、1960年代以降に[[進化主義#新進化主義|新進化主義人類学]]と[[構造主義的マルクス主義]]に取り入れられ、エクホルムらによって器物の流通と婚姻のコントロールにより社会関係が維持・再生産・発展するという視点が現れる{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=75-81}}。 === 威信財システム === [[File:Prestige goods system.svg|400px|thumb|威信財システムのモデル図]] 以上のような成果を踏まえて、1977年に{{仮リンク|ジョナサン・フリードマン|en|Jonathan Friedman}}とマイケル・ローランズは首長制社会から初期国家に至るプロセスを次の5つのステージに設定し、実例に基づき社会進化の説明を試みた{{sfn|辻田淳一郎|2019|p=255-258}}。 * 部族システム - 単系の出自集団（[[リネージ]]）や、共通の祖先をもつと認識する氏族（[[氏族|クラン]]）などに基づき、婚姻や交換を行う社会。 * アジア的国家 - 親族集団同士に序列化が生じ、より上位の親族集団との婚姻関係により社会における階層的位置づけが規定される社会。 * 威信財システム - 各地で首長社会が進展し、各地の上位層が交流を持つことで地域間に威信財の贈与・交換が行われ、同盟的な関係を結ぶ社会。 * 領域国家/都市国家 - 経済的な発展により特定の中心地を核として中央集権的に編成される社会（領域国家）。あるいは経済が独立的に発展する社会（都市国家）。 * 帝国 - 政治的・軍事的に統合される社会。 このうち、威信財システムは[[トンガ]]の首長制社会や中国の[[西周 (王朝)|西周]]代をモデル化したもので、首長制社会が広域に展開する際に地域間の政治的関係が取り結ばれるあり方を示すモデルとして示された{{sfn|辻田淳一郎|2019|p=255-258}}。こうした社会において各地域集団内で威信財を入手できるかは、上位層との婚姻関係にかかっており、上位層から下位層への威信財の贈与と下位層から上位層への貢ぎ物の交換によって政治的同盟関係（上下関係）が維持・再生産される{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=75-81}}。ここでの威信財は、長距離交易によって上位層にもたらされる器物とされた。また、こうした社会構造は、上位層への威信財の供給が不安定になると崩壊する可能性を内包する、流動的な性質があるとされた{{sfn|辻田淳一郎|2019|p=258-259}}。 フリードマンらの社会進化モデルは「[[後成説]]モデル」と呼ばれ、高い評価を受けたが、現在では必ずしも単一のプロセスで社会が進化するとは考えられていない。しかし、考古資料から権力資源を分析することで、世界各地の国家形成を具体的に比較検討しえる手法として評価されている{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=75-81}}。 == 日本での研究 == 1980年代まで日本の古代国家形成論は『[[記紀]]』などの文献史学が中心であったが、文献資料の乏しさや[[史料批判]]の深化により研究が下火となり、他分野との交流も少なく蛸壺化が起きていた。1990年代に[[都出比呂志]]が、考古学と[[文化人類学]]に立脚した初期国家論を提唱すると、それを画期として考古学分野での国家形成論の研究が盛んになる{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=4-7}}{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=8-17}}。そうした中で威信財論は、日本考古学界が好む[[唯物論]]的アプローチと相性が良かった事もあり注目を浴びるようになった{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=81-86}}。 === 日本における研究史 === [[File:Mirror with triangular rim and dragon and tiger design, 4 of 12, Japan, Kofun period, 200s-300s AD, bronze - Tokyo National Museum - Tokyo, Japan - DSC09171.jpg|thumb|古墳時代初期の威信財のひとつ
三角縁神獣鏡]] {{Double image aside|right|稲荷山古墳出土鉄剣 (表面).JPG|110|稲荷山古墳出土鉄剣 (裏面).JPG|110|古墳時代中期の威信財のひとつ
銘文鉄剣（[[稲荷山古墳出土鉄剣]]）}} 威信財論が本格的に日本に導入されるのは1990年代であるが、それ以前から威信財論的な視点はあった。特に1950年代に[[小林行雄]]が提唱した[[三角縁神獣鏡]]の同笵鏡論は威信財論を先取りした成果と評価されている{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=81-82}}{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=95-96}}。小林は、同笵鏡論と伝世鏡論を両輪として[[古墳]]の発生・男系世襲制の成立・[[ヤマト王権|大和政権]]の勢力拡大などの社会変化を[[銅鏡]]を中心に論じた{{sfn|辻田淳一郎|2019|p=116-118}}。この小林の仮説は実証面から批判が相次ぎ、2010年代ではほぼ否定されているが、古墳時代の開始過程の研究に今なお影響を残している{{sfn|辻田淳一郎|2019|p=118-120}}。 欧米での威信財研究を最初に日本の考古学に導入したのは1985年の穴沢咊光の論考である。しかし穴沢の発表はマイナーな媒体であったため普及しなかった{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=81-82}}。 1990年代前半からは、古墳時代を中心に多彩な器物が威信財と見なされた。[[松木武彦]]の[[鉄鏃]]の流通における「威信財重層モデル」など意欲的な提示もあったが、全体的には理論検討や明確な基準もなく、威信財に認定する傾向があった{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=83}}。 1990年代末からは、威信財の用語が濫用される。[[旧石器時代]]の[[石槍]]、[[縄文時代]]の硬玉製大珠・木の葉文浅鉢形土器、[[弥生時代]]の青銅製武器形祭器・[[銅鐸]]、[[古墳時代]]の銅鏡・金銅製品・石製品・武器武具・馬具、[[歴史時代]]の[[陶磁器]]・[[茶器]]など、あらゆる時代の貴重品や高級品が威信財と言い換えられた。その一方で、こうした器物が如何なる機能や役割を果たしたのかという重要な論点が看過された{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=83-86}}。 理論面での検討に先鞭をつけたのは[[河野一隆]]である。河野は威信財を「生産型威信財」と「非生産型威信財」に区別し、弥生時代から古墳時代の社会を考察した。特に「外部から威信財が継続して流入し、威信財が氾濫して社会システムが破綻する事態を、威信財の副葬により消費することで克服した」とする威信財の更新という視点は画期的であった{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=83-86}}{{sfn|辻田淳一郎|2019|p=259-260}}。 2000年代からは日本における威信財論の弱点を克服する動きが見られるようになる。石村智は、流通パターンから「循環する威信財」「分配する威信財」「生産される威信財」に類型化し、[[オセアニア]]諸地域と日本列島の親族関係・生態系・民族事例を検討し、「威信財システムへの依存から脱却することにより社会の階層化が行われる」とする視点を提示した{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=83-86}}{{sfn|辻田淳一郎|2019|p=259-260}}。 [[辻田淳一郎]]は、フリードマンらの威信財システムの枠組みを参考に、日本列島の国家形成プロセスのモデル構築を行った{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=83-86}}。辻田は、威信財を「入手・使用・消費が上位層に独占され、且つそのサイクルが社会的再生産のプロセスと不可分に埋め込まれたもの」と定義し{{sfn|辻田淳一郎|2019|p=258-259}}、銅鏡や[[鉄剣・鉄刀銘文|銘文鉄剣]]が中央政権と地方有力者を繋げる器物であると指摘した{{sfn|辻田淳一郎|2019|p=329-330}}{{sfn|辻田淳一郎|2019|p=333-334}}。さらに、こうした威信財システムが機能した古墳時代においても前期と中期で様相が異なる事や、後期に至ると[[屯倉|ミヤケ制]]・[[国造|国造制]]・[[部民制]]が整い威信財が役割を終えることで社会構造が変化し、古代国家が誕生したと推測した{{sfn|辻田淳一郎|2019|p=371-373}}。 === 威信財研究の問題点 === [[下垣仁志]]は、国家形成論における威信財論の可能性を認めつつも、一方で方法論的な限界や問題点がある事を指摘している{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=86-91}}。 1つ目は物質的資料の残存性である。文化人類学の研究では、威信財が繊維製品や羽毛などの有機物である例が少なくない。『[[魏志倭人伝]]』に記載された[[邪馬台国]]への下賜品も「絳地交龍錦」などの高級織物が大半を占めており、これらが威信財として機能していた可能性は高い。しかし、考古資料は残存性の高い金属器や石製品などの無機物に偏ってしまい、全ての器物の移動を復元することは困難である。また、女性や奴隷の労働力など非物質的な交換を復元することも至難である。こうした非物質的な財は、威信財の贈与に対する貢納として用いられることが多いが、実態が明らかでないため類推に頼らざるを得ず、結果として貢納・反対贈与はしばしば矮小化される{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=86-91}}。 2つ目は器物とそれを保有した人間の関係性が軽視されがちな点である。威信財論は、社会構造の発展・再生産で器物が果たした役割に焦点があてられる事が多い。そうした検証は重要であるが、その反面、あたかも器物が威信をパッキングして集団間を移動するかのように描かれる事が少なくない。しかし実際には所有者が器物の使用、あるいは保管を通して、威信を生成し価値を付与したはずである。こうした威信の生成する行為をなおざりにすると、結果として威信財の交換・移動が行われた要因を軽視することになりうる{{sfn|下垣仁志|2018|p=86-91}}。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 出典 === {{Reflist|20em}} == 参考文献 == ;書籍 * {{Cite book|和書|author=辻田淳一郎 |title=鏡の古代史 |year=2019 |publisher=[[KADOKAWA]] |series=角川選書|isbn=978-4-04-703663-5 |ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=下垣仁志|authorlink=下垣仁志 |title=古墳時代の国家形成 |year=2018 |publisher=吉川弘文館 |isbn=978-4-642-09352-1 |ref=harv}} ;辞典など * “[[コトバンク]]”. [[朝日新聞社]], [[CARTA HOLDINGS|VOYAGE MARKETING]]. ** {{Cite web|和書|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A8%81%E4%BF%A1%E8%B2%A1-1268539 |title=威信財 |accessdate=2021-10-30|ref={{sfnref|コトバンク: 威信財}}}}（『デジタル[[大辞泉]]』ほかより転載）。 == 関連項目 == * [[非市場経済]] * [[同笵鏡論・伝世鏡論]] {{DEFAULTSORT:いしんさい}} [[Category:文化人類学]] [[Category:経済人類学]] [[Category:日本の考古学]] [[Category:先史時代 (日本)]]

revision.text:
'''Ishinzai''' (威信財, {{lang-en|prestige goods}}) refers to valuables that symbolize power, a term used in [[cultural anthropology]]{{sfn|Kotobank: Ishinzai}}. However, the definition of this term is not fixed. In Japan, it is often cited using K. Ekholm's definition as "products that are not necessary for material survival but are essential for maintaining social relationships." It is also frequently used in the sense of "valuable items that indicate prestige ({{lang-en|status symbols}})"{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=90}}{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=255-258}}.
Originally, the term was used in [[economic anthropology]] to refer to artifacts that played a role in chiefdom societies with advanced social stratification. These artifacts confirmed the status of the upper class through gifts and exchanges and established reciprocal relationships. The social system maintained by such artifacts is referred to as the "prestige goods system" and was proposed as a model to explain complex hierarchical societies prior to the formation of early states{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=255-258}}{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=258-259}}. However, in Japanese [[archaeology]], there has been misuse of the term by simply labeling specific artifacts as prestige goods without delving into their social functions or distribution patterns{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=81-86}}.
== Research in Western Studies == === Early Development of Prestige Goods Theory === The origins of prestige goods theory can be traced to the 1922 study of [[kula (exchange)|kula]] by [[Bronisław Malinowski]] and the 1897 study of [[potlatch]] by [[Franz Boas]]. These discoveries advanced the exploration of non-market exchange systems. In 1954, [[Marcel Mauss]] highlighted in his work ''[[The Gift]]'' the concept of "spiritual power" in gift exchange, showing that such exchanges were "total social phenomena" extending beyond economic aspects{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}.
Subsequently, studies of prestige goods in the West were divided into two approaches: those focusing on artifact exchange and those building frameworks for social structure and state formation theories{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=96-97}}. The latter approach was significantly influenced by [[Karl Polanyi]], who, in 1957, proposed the coexistence of two fiscal systems in ancient societies before monetary economies: "prestige good finance" using treasures like prestige goods as payment and "subsistence finance" using food{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}.
This perspective was adopted in the 1960s by [[neoevolutionism|neoevolutionary anthropology]] and [[structural Marxism]], leading researchers like Ekholm to emphasize how the circulation of goods and control over marriage maintained, reproduced, and developed social relationships{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}.
=== Prestige Goods System === [[File:Prestige goods system.svg|400px|thumb|Model of the Prestige Goods System]] Building on these findings, in 1977, {{ill|Jonathan Friedman|en|Jonathan Friedman|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and Michael Rowlands proposed a five-stage process to describe the evolution of chiefdom societies into early states{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=255-258}}: * '''Tribal Systems''' – Societies based on unilineal descent groups ([[lineage]]) or clans that share common ancestry and engage in marriage and exchange. * '''Asiatic States''' – Societies where kinship groups are Ranked, and social positions are determined by marriage ties to higher-Ranked kinship groups. * '''Prestige Goods Systems''' – Chiefdom societies develop across regions, with exchanges and gifts of prestige goods between upper classes forming alliances. * '''Territorial/City-States''' – Economically centralized societies around specific centers or independently developed economies (city-states). * '''Empires''' – Societies unified politically and militarily.
This model demonstrated how prestige goods facilitated alliances between regions and maintained hierarchical relationships through gifts from upper to lower classes and tribute from lower to upper classes{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}. However, it also highlighted the fragility of such societies, as disruptions in the supply of prestige goods could lead to their collapse{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=258-259}}.
== Research in Japan == In Japan, theories of state formation were predominantly based on historical texts like the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' until the 1980s. However, with the decline of documentary sources and the advancement of source criticism, research shifted towards [[archaeology]] and [[cultural anthropology]]{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=4-7}}{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=8-17}}.
=== Development of Prestige Goods Studies === Prestige goods theories were first introduced to Japanese archaeology in 1985 by Hazumi Anazawa. However, its full adoption began in the 1990s, focusing on artifacts like [[triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors]] and iron weapons{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=81-82}}{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=95-96}}.
Key contributions include the "layered model of prestige goods" proposed by [[Takehiko Matsuki]], which explored the distribution of iron arrowheads{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=83}}. However, the term was often overused without sufficient theoretical analysis, leading to the labeling of various items as prestige goods across different historical periods{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=83-86}}.
== Issues in Prestige Goods Research == [[Hitoshi Shitagaki]] has identified limitations in prestige goods theory, including its reliance on material remains and the neglect of non-material exchanges, such as labor and kinship dynamics{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=86-91}}.
== References == {{References help}} === Sources === * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|author=Junichiro Tsujita|title=The Ancient History of Mirrors|year=2019|publisher=[[KADOKAWA]]|isbn=978-4-04-703663-5}} * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|author=Hitoshi Shitagaki|title=State Formation in the Kofun Period|year=2018|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]]|isbn=978-4-642-09352-1}} == See Also == * [[Non-market economy]] * [[Theory of bronze mirror molds]]

'''Ishinzai''' (威信財, {{lang-en|prestige goods}}) refers to valuables that symbolize power, a term used in [[cultural anthropology]]{{sfn|Kotobank: Ishinzai}}. However, the definition of this term is not fixed. In Japan, it is often cited using K. Ekholm's definition as "products that are not necessary for material survival but are essential for maintaining social relationships." It is also frequently used in the sense of "valuable items that indicate prestige ({{lang-en|status symbols}})"{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=90}}{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=255-258}}.
Originally, the term was used in [[economic anthropology]] to refer to artifacts that played a role in chiefdom societies with advanced social stratification. These artifacts confirmed the status of the upper class through gifts and exchanges and established reciprocal relationships. The social system maintained by such artifacts is referred to as the "prestige goods system" and was proposed as a model to explain complex hierarchical societies prior to the formation of early states{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=255-258}}{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=258-259}}. However, in Japanese [[archaeology]], there has been misuse of the term by simply labeling specific artifacts as prestige goods without delving into their social functions or distribution patterns{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=81-86}}.
== Research in Western Studies == === Early Development of Prestige Goods Theory === The origins of prestige goods theory can be traced to the 1922 study of [[kula (exchange)|kula]] by [[Bronisław Malinowski]] and the 1897 study of [[potlatch]] by [[Franz Boas]]. These discoveries advanced the exploration of non-market exchange systems. In 1954, [[Marcel Mauss]] highlighted in his work ''[[The Gift]]'' the concept of "spiritual power" in gift exchange, showing that such exchanges were "total social phenomena" extending beyond economic aspects{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}.
Subsequently, studies of prestige goods in the West were divided into two approaches: those focusing on artifact exchange and those building frameworks for social structure and state formation theories{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=96-97}}. The latter approach was significantly influenced by [[Karl Polanyi]], who, in 1957, proposed the coexistence of two fiscal systems in ancient societies before monetary economies: "prestige good finance" using treasures like prestige goods as payment and "subsistence finance" using food{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}.
This perspective was adopted in the 1960s by [[neoevolutionism|neoevolutionary anthropology]] and [[structural Marxism]], leading researchers like Ekholm to emphasize how the circulation of goods and control over marriage maintained, reproduced, and developed social relationships{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}.
=== Prestige Goods System === [[File:Prestige goods system.svg|400px|thumb|Model of the Prestige Goods System]] Building on these findings, in 1977, {{ill|Jonathan Friedman|en|Jonathan Friedman|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}} and Michael Rowlands proposed a five-stage process to describe the evolution of chiefdom societies into early states{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=255-258}}: * '''Tribal Systems''' – Societies based on unilineal descent groups ([[lineage]]) or clans that share common ancestry and engage in marriage and exchange. * '''Asiatic States''' – Societies where kinship groups are Ranked, and social positions are determined by marriage ties to higher-Ranked kinship groups. * '''Prestige Goods Systems''' – Chiefdom societies develop across regions, with exchanges and gifts of prestige goods between upper classes forming alliances. * '''Territorial/City-States''' – Economically centralized societies around specific centers or independently developed economies (city-states). * '''Empires''' – Societies unified politically and militarily.
This model demonstrated how prestige goods facilitated alliances between regions and maintained hierarchical relationships through gifts from upper to lower classes and tribute from lower to upper classes{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}. However, it also highlighted the fragility of such societies, as disruptions in the supply of prestige goods could lead to their collapse{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=258-259}}.
== Research in Japan == In Japan, theories of state formation were predominantly based on historical texts like the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' until the 1980s. However, with the decline of documentary sources and the advancement of source criticism, research shifted towards [[archaeology]] and [[cultural anthropology]]{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=4-7}}{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=8-17}}.
=== Development of Prestige Goods Studies === Prestige goods theories were first introduced to Japanese archaeology in 1985 by Hazumi Anazawa. However, its full adoption began in the 1990s, focusing on artifacts like [[triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors]] and iron weapons{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=81-82}}{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=95-96}}.
Key contributions include the "layered model of prestige goods" proposed by [[Takehiko Matsuki]], which explored the distribution of iron arrowheads{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=83}}. However, the term was often overused without sufficient theoretical analysis, leading to the labeling of various items as prestige goods across different historical periods{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=83-86}}.
== Issues in Prestige Goods Research == [[Hitoshi Shitagaki]] has identified limitations in prestige goods theory, including its reliance on material remains and the neglect of non-material exchanges, such as labor and kinship dynamics{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=86-91}}.
== References == {{References help}} === Sources === * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|author=Junichiro Tsujita|title=The Ancient History of Mirrors|year=2019|publisher=[[KADOKAWA]]|isbn=978-4-04-703663-5}} * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|author=Hitoshi Shitagaki|title=State Formation in the Kofun Period|year=2018|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]]|isbn=978-4-642-09352-1}} == See Also == * [[Non-market economy]] * [[Theory of bronze mirror molds]]
{{translated page|ja|威信財|version=102441388|comment=Imported full ja history}}

'''Ishinzai''' (威信財, {{lang-en|prestige goods}}) refers to valuables that symbolize power, a term used in [[cultural anthropology]]{{sfn|Kotobank: Ishinzai}}. However, the definition of this term is not fixed. In Japan, it is often cited using K. Ekholm's definition as "products that are not necessary for material survival but are essential for maintaining social relationships." It is also frequently used in the sense of "valuable items that indicate prestige ({{lang-en|status symbols}})"{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=90}}{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=255-258}}.
Originally, the term was used in [[economic anthropology]] to refer to artifacts that played a role in chiefdom societies with advanced social stratification. These artifacts confirmed the status of the upper class through gifts and exchanges and established reciprocal relationships. The social system maintained by such artifacts is referred to as the "prestige goods system" and was proposed as a model to explain complex hierarchical societies prior to the formation of early states{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=255-258}}{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=258-259}}. However, in Japanese [[archaeology]], there has been misuse of the term by simply labeling specific artifacts as prestige goods without delving into their social functions or distribution patterns{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=81-86}}.

This model demonstrated how prestige goods facilitated alliances between regions and maintained hierarchical relationships through gifts from upper to lower classes and tribute from lower to upper classes{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}. However, it also highlighted the fragility of such societies, as disruptions in the supply of prestige goods could lead to their collapse{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=258-259}}.

However, with the decline of documentary sources and the advancement of source criticism, research shifted towards [[archaeology]] and [[cultural anthropology]]{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=4-7}}{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=8-17}}.
=== Development of Prestige Goods Studies === Prestige goods theories were first introduced to Japanese archaeology in 1985 by Hazumi Anazawa. However, its full adoption began in the 1990s, focusing on artifacts like [[triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors]] and iron weapons{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=81-82}}{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=95-96}}.
Key contributions include the "layered model of prestige goods" proposed by [[Takehiko Matsuki]], which explored the distribution of iron arrowheads{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=83}}. However, the term was often overused without sufficient theoretical analysis, leading to the labeling of various items as prestige goods across different historical periods{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=83-86}}.
== Issues in Prestige Goods Research == [[Hitoshi Shitagaki]] has identified limitations in prestige goods theory, including its reliance on material remains and the neglect of non-material exchanges, such as labor and kinship dynamics{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=86-91}}.

However, the term was often overused without sufficient theoretical analysis, leading to the labeling of various items as prestige goods across different historical periods{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=83-86}}.
== Issues in Prestige Goods Research == [[Hitoshi Shitagaki]] has identified limitations in prestige goods theory, including its reliance on material remains and the neglect of non-material exchanges, such as labor and kinship dynamics{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=86-91}}.
== References == {{References help}} === Sources === * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|author=Junichiro Tsujita|title=The Ancient History of Mirrors|year=2019|publisher=[[KADOKAWA]]|isbn=978-4-04-703663-5}} * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|author=Hitoshi Shitagaki|title=State Formation in the Kofun Period|year=2018|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]]|isbn=978-4-642-09352-1}} == See Also == * [[Non-market economy]] * [[Theory of bronze mirror molds]]

revision.comment: Bot: remove sole member from [[Category:Prestige goods]]
revision.text:
'''Ishinzai''' (威信財, {{lang-en|prestige goods}}) refers to valuables that symbolize power, a term used in [[cultural anthropology]]{{sfn|Kotobank: Ishinzai}}. However, the definition of this term is not fixed. In Japan, it is often cited using K. Ekholm's definition as "products that are not necessary for material survival but are essential for maintaining social relationships." It is also frequently used in the sense of "valuable items that indicate prestige ({{lang-en|status symbols}})"{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=90}}{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=255-258}}.
Originally, the term was used in [[economic anthropology]] to refer to artifacts that played a role in chiefdom societies with advanced social stratification. These artifacts confirmed the status of the upper class through gifts and exchanges and established reciprocal relationships. The social system maintained by such artifacts is referred to as the "prestige goods system" and was proposed as a model to explain complex hierarchical societies prior to the formation of early states{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=255-258}}{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=258-259}}. However, in Japanese [[archaeology]], there has been misuse of the term by simply labeling specific artifacts as prestige goods without delving into their social functions or distribution patterns{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=81-86}}.
== Research in Western Studies == === Early Development of Prestige Goods Theory === The origins of prestige goods theory can be traced to the 1922 study of [[kula (exchange)|kula]] by [[Bronisław Malinowski]] and the 1897 study of [[potlatch]] by [[Franz Boas]]. These discoveries advanced the exploration of non-market exchange systems. In 1954, [[Marcel Mauss]] highlighted in his work ''[[The Gift]]'' the concept of "spiritual power" in gift exchange, showing that such exchanges were "total social phenomena" extending beyond economic aspects{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}.
Subsequently, studies of prestige goods in the West were divided into two approaches: those focusing on artifact exchange and those building frameworks for social structure and state formation theories{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=96-97}}. The latter approach was significantly influenced by [[Karl Polanyi]], who, in 1957, proposed the coexistence of two fiscal systems in ancient societies before monetary economies: "prestige good finance" using treasures like prestige goods as payment and "subsistence finance" using food{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}.
This perspective was adopted in the 1960s by [[neoevolutionism|neoevolutionary anthropology]] and [[structural Marxism]], leading researchers like Ekholm to emphasize how the circulation of goods and control over marriage maintained, reproduced, and developed social relationships{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}.
=== Prestige Goods System === [[File:Prestige goods system.svg|400px|thumb|Model of the Prestige Goods System]] Building on these findings, in 1977, {{ill|Jonathan Friedman|en|Jonathan Friedman|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jonathan Friedman|qq=}} and Michael Rowlands proposed a five-stage process to describe the evolution of chiefdom societies into early states{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=255-258}}: * '''Tribal Systems''' – Societies based on unilineal descent groups ([[lineage]]) or clans that share common ancestry and engage in marriage and exchange. * '''Asiatic States''' – Societies where kinship groups are Ranked, and social positions are determined by marriage ties to higher-Ranked kinship groups. * '''Prestige Goods Systems''' – Chiefdom societies develop across regions, with exchanges and gifts of prestige goods between upper classes forming alliances. * '''Territorial/City-States''' – Economically centralized societies around specific centers or independently developed economies (city-states). * '''Empires''' – Societies unified politically and militarily.
This model demonstrated how prestige goods facilitated alliances between regions and maintained hierarchical relationships through gifts from upper to lower classes and tribute from lower to upper classes{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}. However, it also highlighted the fragility of such societies, as disruptions in the supply of prestige goods could lead to their collapse{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=258-259}}.
== Research in Japan == In Japan, theories of state formation were predominantly based on historical texts like the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' until the 1980s. However, with the decline of documentary sources and the advancement of source criticism, research shifted towards [[archaeology]] and [[cultural anthropology]]{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=4-7}}{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=8-17}}.
=== Development of Prestige Goods Studies === Prestige goods theories were first introduced to Japanese archaeology in 1985 by Hazumi Anazawa. However, its full adoption began in the 1990s, focusing on artifacts like [[triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors]] and iron weapons{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=81-82}}{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=95-96}}.
Key contributions include the "layered model of prestige goods" proposed by [[Takehiko Matsuki]], which explored the distribution of iron arrowheads{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=83}}. However, the term was often overused without sufficient theoretical analysis, leading to the labeling of various items as prestige goods across different historical periods{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=83-86}}.
== Issues in Prestige Goods Research == [[Hitoshi Shitagaki]] has identified limitations in prestige goods theory, including its reliance on material remains and the neglect of non-material exchanges, such as labor and kinship dynamics{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=86-91}}.
== References == {{References help}} === Sources === * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|author=Junichiro Tsujita|title=The Ancient History of Mirrors|year=2019|publisher=[[KADOKAWA]]|isbn=978-4-04-703663-5}} * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|author=Hitoshi Shitagaki|title=State Formation in the Kofun Period|year=2018|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]]|isbn=978-4-642-09352-1}} == See Also == * [[Non-market economy]] * [[Theory of bronze mirror molds]]
{{translated page|ja|威信財|version=102441388|comment=Imported full ja history}}

== Research in Western Studies == === Early Development of Prestige Goods Theory === The origins of prestige goods theory can be traced to the 1922 study of [[kula (exchange)|kula]] by [[Bronisław Malinowski]] and the 1897 study of [[potlatch]] by [[Franz Boas]]. These discoveries advanced the exploration of non-market exchange systems. In 1954, [[Marcel Mauss]] highlighted in his work ''[[The Gift]]'' the concept of "spiritual power" in gift exchange, showing that such exchanges were "total social phenomena" extending beyond economic aspects{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}.
Subsequently, studies of prestige goods in the West were divided into two approaches: those focusing on artifact exchange and those building frameworks for social structure and state formation theories{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=96-97}}. The latter approach was significantly influenced by [[Karl Polanyi]], who, in 1957, proposed the coexistence of two fiscal systems in ancient societies before monetary economies: "prestige good finance" using treasures like prestige goods as payment and "subsistence finance" using food{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}.
This perspective was adopted in the 1960s by [[neoevolutionism|neoevolutionary anthropology]] and [[structural Marxism]], leading researchers like Ekholm to emphasize how the circulation of goods and control over marriage maintained, reproduced, and developed social relationships{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}.
=== Prestige Goods System === [[File:Prestige goods system.svg|400px|thumb|Model of the Prestige Goods System]] Building on these findings, in 1977, {{ill|Jonathan Friedman|en|Jonathan Friedman|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jonathan Friedman|qq=}} and Michael Rowlands proposed a five-stage process to describe the evolution of chiefdom societies into early states{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=255-258}}: * '''Tribal Systems''' – Societies based on unilineal descent groups ([[lineage]]) or clans that share common ancestry and engage in marriage and exchange. * '''Asiatic States''' – Societies where kinship groups are Ranked, and social positions are determined by marriage ties to higher-Ranked kinship groups. * '''Prestige Goods Systems''' – Chiefdom societies develop across regions, with exchanges and gifts of prestige goods between upper classes forming alliances. * '''Territorial/City-States''' – Economically centralized societies around specific centers or independently developed economies (city-states). * '''Empires''' – Societies unified politically and militarily.
This model demonstrated how prestige goods facilitated alliances between regions and maintained hierarchical relationships through gifts from upper to lower classes and tribute from lower to upper classes{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}. However, it also highlighted the fragility of such societies, as disruptions in the supply of prestige goods could lead to their collapse{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=258-259}}.
== Research in Japan == In Japan, theories of state formation were predominantly based on historical texts like the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' until the 1980s. However, with the decline of documentary sources and the advancement of source criticism, research shifted towards [[archaeology]] and [[cultural anthropology]]{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=4-7}}{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=8-17}}.
=== Development of Prestige Goods Studies === Prestige goods theories were first introduced to Japanese archaeology in 1985 by Hazumi Anazawa. However, its full adoption began in the 1990s, focusing on artifacts like [[Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|Triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors]] and iron weapons{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=81-82}}{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=95-96}}.
Key contributions include the "layered model of prestige goods" proposed by [[Takehiko Matsuki]], which explored the distribution of iron arrowheads{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=83}}. However, the term was often overused without sufficient theoretical analysis, leading to the labeling of various items as prestige goods across different historical periods{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=83-86}}.
== Issues in Prestige Goods Research == [[Hitoshi Shitagaki]] has identified limitations in prestige goods theory, including its reliance on material remains and the neglect of non-material exchanges, such as labor and kinship dynamics{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=86-91}}.
== References == {{References help}} === Sources === * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|author=Junichiro Tsujita|title=The Ancient History of Mirrors|year=2019|publisher=[[KADOKAWA]]|isbn=978-4-04-703663-5}} * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|author=Hitoshi Shitagaki|title=State Formation in the Kofun Period|year=2018|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]]|isbn=978-4-642-09352-1}} == See Also == * [[Non-market economy]] * [[Theory of bronze mirror molds]]
{{translated page|ja|威信財|version=102441388|comment=Imported full ja history}}

'''Ishinzai''' (威信財, {{lang-en|prestige goods}}) refers to valuables that symbolize power, a term used in [[cultural anthropology]]{{sfn|Kotobank: Ishinzai}}. However, the definition of this term is not fixed. In Japan, it is often cited using K. Ekholm's definition as "products that are not necessary for material survival but are essential for maintaining social relationships." It is also frequently used in the sense of "valuable items that indicate prestige ({{lang-en|status symbols}})"{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=90}}{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=255-258}}.
Originally, the term was used in [[economic anthropology]] to refer to artifacts that played a role in chiefdom societies with advanced social stratification. These artifacts confirmed the status of the upper class through gifts and exchanges and established reciprocal relationships. The social system maintained by such artifacts is referred to as the "prestige goods system" and was proposed as a model to explain complex hierarchical societies prior to the formation of early states{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=255-258}}{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=258-259}}. However, in Japanese [[archaeology]], there has been misuse of the term by simply labeling specific artifacts as prestige goods without delving into their social functions or distribution patterns{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=81-86}}.
== Research in Western Studies == === Early Development of Prestige Goods Theory === The origins of prestige goods theory can be traced to the 1922 study of [[kula (exchange)|kula]] by [[Bronisław Malinowski]] and the 1897 study of [[potlatch]] by [[Franz Boas]]. These discoveries advanced the exploration of non-market exchange systems. In 1954, [[Marcel Mauss]] highlighted in his work ''[[The Gift]]'' the concept of "spiritual power" in gift exchange, showing that such exchanges were "total social phenomena" extending beyond economic aspects{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}.
Subsequently, studies of prestige goods in the West were divided into two approaches: those focusing on artifact exchange and those building frameworks for social structure and state formation theories{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=96-97}}. The latter approach was significantly influenced by [[Karl Polanyi]], who, in 1957, proposed the coexistence of two fiscal systems in ancient societies before monetary economies: "prestige good finance" using treasures like prestige goods as payment and "subsistence finance" using food{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}.
This perspective was adopted in the 1960s by [[neoevolutionism|neoevolutionary anthropology]] and [[structural Marxism]], leading researchers like Ekholm to emphasize how the circulation of goods and control over marriage maintained, reproduced, and developed social relationships{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}.

This model demonstrated how prestige goods facilitated alliances between regions and maintained hierarchical relationships through gifts from upper to lower classes and tribute from lower to upper classes{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}. However, it also highlighted the fragility of such societies, as disruptions in the supply of prestige goods could lead to their collapse{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=258-259}}.
== Research in Japan == In Japan, theories of state formation were predominantly based on historical texts like the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' until the 1980s. However, with the decline of documentary sources and the advancement of source criticism, research shifted towards [[archaeology]] and [[cultural anthropology]]{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=4-7}}{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=8-17}}.
=== Development of Prestige Goods Studies === Prestige goods theories were first introduced to Japanese archaeology in 1985 by Hazumi Anazawa. However, its full adoption began in the 1990s, focusing on artifacts like [[Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|Triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors]] and iron weapons{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=81-82}}{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=95-96}}.
Key contributions include the "layered model of prestige goods" proposed by [[Takehiko Matsuki]], which explored the distribution of iron arrowheads{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=83}}. However, the term was often overused without sufficient theoretical analysis, leading to the labeling of various items as prestige goods across different historical periods{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=83-86}}.
== Issues in Prestige Goods Research == [[Hitoshi Shitagaki]] has identified limitations in prestige goods theory, including its reliance on material remains and the neglect of non-material exchanges, such as labor and kinship dynamics{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=86-91}}.
== References == {{References help}} === Sources === * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|author=Junichiro Tsujita|title=The Ancient History of Mirrors|year=2019|publisher=[[KADOKAWA]]|isbn=978-4-04-703663-5}} * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|author=Hitoshi Shitagaki|title=State Formation in the Kofun Period|year=2018|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]]|isbn=978-4-642-09352-1}} == See Also == * [[Non-market economy]] * [[Theory of bronze mirror molds]]

However, its full adoption began in the 1990s, focusing on artifacts like [[Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|Triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors]] and iron weapons{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=81-82}}{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=95-96}}.
Key contributions include the "layered model of prestige goods" proposed by [[Takehiko Matsuki]], which explored the distribution of iron arrowheads{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=83}}. However, the term was often overused without sufficient theoretical analysis, leading to the labeling of various items as prestige goods across different historical periods{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=83-86}}.
== Issues in Prestige Goods Research == [[Hitoshi Shitagaki]] has identified limitations in prestige goods theory, including its reliance on material remains and the neglect of non-material exchanges, such as labor and kinship dynamics{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=86-91}}.
== References == {{References help}} === Sources === * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|author=Junichiro Tsujita|title=The Ancient History of Mirrors|year=2019|publisher=[[KADOKAWA]]|isbn=978-4-04-703663-5}} * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|author=Hitoshi Shitagaki|title=State Formation in the Kofun Period|year=2018|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]]|isbn=978-4-642-09352-1}} == See Also == * [[Non-market economy]] * [[Theory of bronze mirror molds]]

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Peripheral articles
revision.text:
'''Ishinzai''' (威信財, {{lang-en|prestige goods}}) refers to valuables that symbolize power, a term used in [[cultural anthropology]]{{sfn|Kotobank: Ishinzai}}. However, the definition of this term is not fixed. In Japan, it is often cited using K. Ekholm's definition as "products that are not necessary for material survival but are essential for maintaining social relationships." It is also frequently used in the sense of "valuable items that indicate prestige ({{lang-en|status symbols}})"{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=90}}{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=255-258}}.
Originally, the term was used in [[:en:economic anthropology|economic anthropology]] to refer to artifacts that played a role in chiefdom societies with advanced social stratification. These artifacts confirmed the status of the upper class through gifts and exchanges and established reciprocal relationships. The social system maintained by such artifacts is referred to as the "prestige goods system" and was proposed as a model to explain complex hierarchical societies prior to the formation of early states{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=255-258}}{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=258-259}}. However, in Japanese [[archaeology]], there has been misuse of the term by simply labeling specific artifacts as prestige goods without delving into their social functions or distribution patterns{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=81-86}}.
== Research in Western Studies == === Early Development of Prestige Goods Theory === The origins of prestige goods theory can be traced to the 1922 study of [[kula (exchange)|kula]] by [[Bronisław Malinowski]] and the 1897 study of [[potlatch]] by [[:en:Franz Boas|Franz Boas]]. These discoveries advanced the exploration of non-market exchange systems. In 1954, [[Marcel Mauss]] highlighted in his work ''[[The Gift]]'' the concept of "spiritual power" in gift exchange, showing that such exchanges were "total social phenomena" extending beyond economic aspects{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}.
Subsequently, studies of prestige goods in the West were divided into two approaches: those focusing on artifact exchange and those building frameworks for social structure and state formation theories{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=96-97}}. The latter approach was significantly influenced by [[Karl Polanyi]], who, in 1957, proposed the coexistence of two fiscal systems in ancient societies before monetary economies: "prestige good finance" using treasures like prestige goods as payment and "subsistence finance" using food{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}.
This perspective was adopted in the 1960s by [[neoevolutionism|neoevolutionary anthropology]] and [[structural Marxism]], leading researchers like Ekholm to emphasize how the circulation of goods and control over marriage maintained, reproduced, and developed social relationships{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}.
=== Prestige Goods System === [[File:Prestige goods system.svg|400px|thumb|Model of the Prestige Goods System]] Building on these findings, in 1977, {{ill|Jonathan Friedman|en|Jonathan Friedman|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jonathan Friedman|qq=}} and Michael Rowlands proposed a five-stage process to describe the evolution of chiefdom societies into early states{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=255-258}}: * '''Tribal Systems''' – Societies based on unilineal descent groups ([[lineage]]) or clans that share common ancestry and engage in marriage and exchange. * '''Asiatic States''' – Societies where kinship groups are Ranked, and social positions are determined by marriage ties to higher-Ranked kinship groups. * '''Prestige Goods Systems''' – Chiefdom societies develop across regions, with exchanges and gifts of prestige goods between upper classes forming alliances. * '''Territorial/City-States''' – Economically centralized societies around specific centers or independently developed economies (city-states). * '''Empires''' – Societies unified politically and militarily.
This model demonstrated how prestige goods facilitated alliances between regions and maintained hierarchical relationships through gifts from upper to lower classes and tribute from lower to upper classes{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}. However, it also highlighted the fragility of such societies, as disruptions in the supply of prestige goods could lead to their collapse{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=258-259}}.
== Research in Japan == In Japan, theories of state formation were predominantly based on historical texts like the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' until the 1980s. However, with the decline of documentary sources and the advancement of source criticism, research shifted towards [[archaeology]] and [[cultural anthropology]]{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=4-7}}{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=8-17}}.
=== Development of Prestige Goods Studies === Prestige goods theories were first introduced to Japanese archaeology in 1985 by Hazumi Anazawa. However, its full adoption began in the 1990s, focusing on artifacts like [[Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|Triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors]] and iron weapons{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=81-82}}{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=95-96}}.
Key contributions include the "layered model of prestige goods" proposed by [[Takehiko Matsuki]], which explored the distribution of iron arrowheads{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=83}}. However, the term was often overused without sufficient theoretical analysis, leading to the labeling of various items as prestige goods across different historical periods{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=83-86}}.
== Issues in Prestige Goods Research == [[Hitoshi Shitagaki]] has identified limitations in prestige goods theory, including its reliance on material remains and the neglect of non-material exchanges, such as labor and kinship dynamics{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=86-91}}.
== References == {{References help}} === Sources === * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|author=Junichiro Tsujita|title=The Ancient History of Mirrors|year=2019|publisher=[[KADOKAWA]]|isbn=978-4-04-703663-5}} * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|author=Hitoshi Shitagaki|title=State Formation in the Kofun Period|year=2018|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]]|isbn=978-4-642-09352-1}} == See Also == * [[Non-market economy]] * [[Theory of bronze mirror molds]]
{{translated page|ja|威信財|version=102441388|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Category:Completion tier 2]]
}}

Ekholm's definition as "products that are not necessary for material survival but are essential for maintaining social relationships." It is also frequently used in the sense of "valuable items that indicate prestige ({{lang-en|status symbols}})"{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=90}}{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=255-258}}.
Originally, the term was used in [[:en:economic anthropology|economic anthropology]] to refer to artifacts that played a role in chiefdom societies with advanced social stratification. These artifacts confirmed the status of the upper class through gifts and exchanges and established reciprocal relationships. The social system maintained by such artifacts is referred to as the "prestige goods system" and was proposed as a model to explain complex hierarchical societies prior to the formation of early states{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=255-258}}{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=258-259}}. However, in Japanese [[:en:Archaeology|Archaeology]], there has been misuse of the term by simply labeling specific artifacts as prestige goods without delving into their social functions or distribution patterns{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=81-86}}.
== Research in Western Studies == === Early Development of Prestige Goods Theory === The origins of prestige goods theory can be traced to the 1922 study of [[kula (exchange)|kula]] by [[:en:Bronisław Malinowski|Bronisław Malinowski]] and the 1897 study of [[potlatch]] by [[:en:Franz Boas|Franz Boas]]. These discoveries advanced the exploration of non-market exchange systems. In 1954, [[Marcel Mauss]] highlighted in his work ''[[The Gift]]'' the concept of "spiritual power" in gift exchange, showing that such exchanges were "total social phenomena" extending beyond economic aspects{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}.
Subsequently, studies of prestige goods in the West were divided into two approaches: those focusing on artifact exchange and those building frameworks for social structure and state formation theories{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=96-97}}. The latter approach was significantly influenced by [[:en:Karl Polanyi|Karl Polanyi]], who, in 1957, proposed the coexistence of two fiscal systems in ancient societies before monetary economies: "prestige good finance" using treasures like prestige goods as payment and "subsistence finance" using food{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}.
This perspective was adopted in the 1960s by [[neoevolutionism|neoevolutionary anthropology]] and [[structural Marxism]], leading researchers like Ekholm to emphasize how the circulation of goods and control over marriage maintained, reproduced, and developed social relationships{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}.
=== Prestige Goods System === [[File:Prestige goods system.svg|400px|thumb|Model of the Prestige Goods System]] Building on these findings, in 1977, {{ill|Jonathan Friedman|en|Jonathan Friedman|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jonathan Friedman|qq=}} and Michael Rowlands proposed a five-stage process to describe the evolution of chiefdom societies into early states{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=255-258}}: * '''Tribal Systems''' – Societies based on unilineal descent groups ([[lineage]]) or clans that share common ancestry and engage in marriage and exchange. * '''Asiatic States''' – Societies where kinship groups are Ranked, and social positions are determined by marriage ties to higher-Ranked kinship groups. * '''Prestige Goods Systems''' – Chiefdom societies develop across regions, with exchanges and gifts of prestige goods between upper classes forming alliances. * '''Territorial/City-States''' – Economically centralized societies around specific centers or independently developed economies (city-states). * '''Empires''' – Societies unified politically and militarily.
This model demonstrated how prestige goods facilitated alliances between regions and maintained hierarchical relationships through gifts from upper to lower classes and tribute from lower to upper classes{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}. However, it also highlighted the fragility of such societies, as disruptions in the supply of prestige goods could lead to their collapse{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=258-259}}.
== Research in Japan == In Japan, theories of state formation were predominantly based on historical texts like the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' until the 1980s. However, with the decline of documentary sources and the advancement of source criticism, research shifted towards [[:en:Archaeology|Archaeology]] and [[:en:Cultural anthropology|Cultural anthropology]]{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=4-7}}{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=8-17}}.
=== Development of Prestige Goods Studies === Prestige goods theories were first introduced to Japanese archaeology in 1985 by Hazumi Anazawa. However, its full adoption began in the 1990s, focusing on artifacts like [[Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|Triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors]] and iron weapons{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=81-82}}{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=95-96}}.
Key contributions include the "layered model of prestige goods" proposed by [[Takehiko Matsuki]], which explored the distribution of iron arrowheads{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=83}}. However, the term was often overused without sufficient theoretical analysis, leading to the labeling of various items as prestige goods across different historical periods{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=83-86}}.
== Issues in Prestige Goods Research == [[Hitoshi Shitagaki]] has identified limitations in prestige goods theory, including its reliance on material remains and the neglect of non-material exchanges, such as labor and kinship dynamics{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=86-91}}.
== References == {{References help}} === Sources === * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|author=Junichiro Tsujita|title=The Ancient History of Mirrors|year=2019|publisher=[[KADOKAWA]]|isbn=978-4-04-703663-5}} * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|author=Hitoshi Shitagaki|title=State Formation in the Kofun Period|year=2018|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]]|isbn=978-4-642-09352-1}} == See Also == * [[Non-market economy]] * [[Theory of bronze mirror molds]]

}}

The latter approach was significantly influenced by [[:en:Karl Polanyi|Karl Polanyi]], who, in 1957, proposed the coexistence of two fiscal systems in ancient societies before monetary economies: "prestige good finance" using treasures like prestige goods as payment and "subsistence finance" using food{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}.

=== Prestige Goods System === [[File:Prestige goods system.svg|400px|thumb|Model of the Prestige Goods System]] Building on these findings, in 1977, {{ill|Jonathan Friedman|en|Jonathan Friedman|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jonathan Friedman|qq=}} and Michael Rowlands proposed a five-stage process to describe the evolution of chiefdom societies into early states{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=255-258}}: * '''Tribal Systems''' – Societies based on unilineal descent groups ([[:en:Lineage|Lineage]]) or clans that share common ancestry and engage in marriage and exchange. * '''Asiatic States''' – Societies where kinship groups are Ranked, and social positions are determined by marriage ties to higher-Ranked kinship groups. * '''Prestige Goods Systems''' – Chiefdom societies develop across regions, with exchanges and gifts of prestige goods between upper classes forming alliances. * '''Territorial/City-States''' – Economically centralized societies around specific centers or independently developed economies (city-states). * '''Empires''' – Societies unified politically and militarily.
This model demonstrated how prestige goods facilitated alliances between regions and maintained hierarchical relationships through gifts from upper to lower classes and tribute from lower to upper classes{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}. However, it also highlighted the fragility of such societies, as disruptions in the supply of prestige goods could lead to their collapse{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=258-259}}.
== Research in Japan == In Japan, theories of state formation were predominantly based on historical texts like the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' until the 1980s. However, with the decline of documentary sources and the advancement of source criticism, research shifted towards [[:en:Archaeology|Archaeology]] and [[:en:Cultural anthropology|Cultural anthropology]]{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=4-7}}{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=8-17}}.
=== Development of Prestige Goods Studies === Prestige goods theories were first introduced to Japanese archaeology in 1985 by Hazumi Anazawa. However, its full adoption began in the 1990s, focusing on artifacts like [[Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|Triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors]] and iron weapons{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=81-82}}{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=95-96}}.
Key contributions include the "layered model of prestige goods" proposed by [[Takehiko Matsuki]], which explored the distribution of iron arrowheads{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=83}}. However, the term was often overused without sufficient theoretical analysis, leading to the labeling of various items as prestige goods across different historical periods{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=83-86}}.
== Issues in Prestige Goods Research == [[Hitoshi Shitagaki]] has identified limitations in prestige goods theory, including its reliance on material remains and the neglect of non-material exchanges, such as labor and kinship dynamics{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=86-91}}.
== References == {{References help}} === Sources === * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|author=Junichiro Tsujita|title=The Ancient History of Mirrors|year=2019|publisher=[[KADOKAWA]]|isbn=978-4-04-703663-5}} * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|author=Hitoshi Shitagaki|title=State Formation in the Kofun Period|year=2018|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]]|isbn=978-4-642-09352-1}} == See Also == * [[Non-market economy]] * [[Theory of bronze mirror molds]]

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
'''Ishinzai''' (威信財, {{lang-en|prestige goods}}) refers to valuables that symbolize power, a term used in [[:en:Cultural anthropology|Cultural anthropology]]{{sfn|Kotobank: Ishinzai}}. However, the definition of this term is not fixed. In Japan, it is often cited using K. Ekholm's definition as "products that are not necessary for material survival but are essential for maintaining social relationships." It is also frequently used in the sense of "valuable items that indicate prestige ({{lang-en|status symbols}})"{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=90}}{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=255-258}}.
Originally, the term was used in [[:en:economic anthropology|economic anthropology]] to refer to artifacts that played a role in chiefdom societies with advanced social stratification. These artifacts confirmed the status of the upper class through gifts and exchanges and established reciprocal relationships. The social system maintained by such artifacts is referred to as the "prestige goods system" and was proposed as a model to explain complex hierarchical societies prior to the formation of early states{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=255-258}}{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=258-259}}. However, in Japanese [[:en:Archaeology|Archaeology]], there has been misuse of the term by simply labeling specific artifacts as prestige goods without delving into their social functions or distribution patterns{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=81-86}}.
== Research in Western Studies == === Early Development of Prestige Goods Theory === The origins of prestige goods theory can be traced to the 1922 study of [[kula (exchange)|kula]] by [[:en:Bronisław Malinowski|Bronisław Malinowski]] and the 1897 study of [[:en:potlatch|potlatch]] by [[:en:Franz Boas|Franz Boas]]. These discoveries advanced the exploration of non-market exchange systems. In 1954, [[:en:Marcel Mauss|Marcel Mauss]] highlighted in his work ''[[:en:The Gift|The Gift]]'' the concept of "spiritual power" in gift exchange, showing that such exchanges were "total social phenomena" extending beyond economic aspects{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}.
Subsequently, studies of prestige goods in the West were divided into two approaches: those focusing on artifact exchange and those building frameworks for social structure and state formation theories{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=96-97}}. The latter approach was significantly influenced by [[:en:Karl Polanyi|Karl Polanyi]], who, in 1957, proposed the coexistence of two fiscal systems in ancient societies before monetary economies: "prestige good finance" using treasures like prestige goods as payment and "subsistence finance" using food{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}.
This perspective was adopted in the 1960s by [[:en:neoevolutionism|neoevolutionary anthropology]] and [[structural Marxism]], leading researchers like Ekholm to emphasize how the circulation of goods and control over marriage maintained, reproduced, and developed social relationships{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}.
=== Prestige Goods System === [[File:Prestige goods system.svg|400px|thumb|Model of the Prestige Goods System]] Building on these findings, in 1977, {{ill|Jonathan Friedman|en|Jonathan Friedman|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jonathan Friedman|qq=}} and Michael Rowlands proposed a five-stage process to describe the evolution of chiefdom societies into early states{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=255-258}}: * '''Tribal Systems''' – Societies based on unilineal descent groups ([[:en:Lineage|Lineage]]) or clans that share common ancestry and engage in marriage and exchange. * '''Asiatic States''' – Societies where kinship groups are Ranked, and social positions are determined by marriage ties to higher-Ranked kinship groups. * '''Prestige Goods Systems''' – Chiefdom societies develop across regions, with exchanges and gifts of prestige goods between upper classes forming alliances. * '''Territorial/City-States''' – Economically centralized societies around specific centers or independently developed economies (city-states). * '''Empires''' – Societies unified politically and militarily.
This model demonstrated how prestige goods facilitated alliances between regions and maintained hierarchical relationships through gifts from upper to lower classes and tribute from lower to upper classes{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}. However, it also highlighted the fragility of such societies, as disruptions in the supply of prestige goods could lead to their collapse{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=258-259}}.
== Research in Japan == In Japan, theories of state formation were predominantly based on historical texts like the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' until the 1980s. However, with the decline of documentary sources and the advancement of source criticism, research shifted towards [[:en:Archaeology|Archaeology]] and [[:en:Cultural anthropology|Cultural anthropology]]{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=4-7}}{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=8-17}}.
=== Development of Prestige Goods Studies === Prestige goods theories were first introduced to Japanese archaeology in 1985 by Hazumi Anazawa. However, its full adoption began in the 1990s, focusing on artifacts like [[Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|Triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors]] and iron weapons{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=81-82}}{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=95-96}}.
Key contributions include the "layered model of prestige goods" proposed by [[Takehiko Matsuki]], which explored the distribution of iron arrowheads{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=83}}. However, the term was often overused without sufficient theoretical analysis, leading to the labeling of various items as prestige goods across different historical periods{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=83-86}}.
== Issues in Prestige Goods Research == [[Hitoshi Shitagaki]] has identified limitations in prestige goods theory, including its reliance on material remains and the neglect of non-material exchanges, such as labor and kinship dynamics{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=86-91}}.
== References == {{References help}} === Sources === * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|author=Junichiro Tsujita|title=The Ancient History of Mirrors|year=2019|publisher=[[KADOKAWA]]|isbn=978-4-04-703663-5}} * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|author=Hitoshi Shitagaki|title=State Formation in the Kofun Period|year=2018|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]]|isbn=978-4-642-09352-1}} == See Also == * [[Non-market economy]] * [[Theory of bronze mirror molds]]

This perspective was adopted in the 1960s by [[:en:neoevolutionism|neoevolutionary anthropology]] and [[:en:structural Marxism|structural Marxism]], leading researchers like Ekholm to emphasize how the circulation of goods and control over marriage maintained, reproduced, and developed social relationships{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}.
=== Prestige Goods System === [[File:Prestige goods system.svg|400px|thumb|Model of the Prestige Goods System]] Building on these findings, in 1977, {{ill|Jonathan Friedman|en|Jonathan Friedman|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jonathan Friedman|qq=}} and Michael Rowlands proposed a five-stage process to describe the evolution of chiefdom societies into early states{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=255-258}}: * '''Tribal Systems''' – Societies based on unilineal descent groups ([[:en:Lineage|Lineage]]) or clans that share common ancestry and engage in marriage and exchange. * '''Asiatic States''' – Societies where kinship groups are Ranked, and social positions are determined by marriage ties to higher-Ranked kinship groups. * '''Prestige Goods Systems''' – Chiefdom societies develop across regions, with exchanges and gifts of prestige goods between upper classes forming alliances. * '''Territorial/City-States''' – Economically centralized societies around specific centers or independently developed economies (city-states). * '''Empires''' – Societies unified politically and militarily.
This model demonstrated how prestige goods facilitated alliances between regions and maintained hierarchical relationships through gifts from upper to lower classes and tribute from lower to upper classes{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}. However, it also highlighted the fragility of such societies, as disruptions in the supply of prestige goods could lead to their collapse{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=258-259}}.
== Research in Japan == In Japan, theories of state formation were predominantly based on historical texts like the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' until the 1980s. However, with the decline of documentary sources and the advancement of source criticism, research shifted towards [[:en:Archaeology|Archaeology]] and [[:en:Cultural anthropology|Cultural anthropology]]{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=4-7}}{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=8-17}}.
=== Development of Prestige Goods Studies === Prestige goods theories were first introduced to Japanese archaeology in 1985 by Hazumi Anazawa. However, its full adoption began in the 1990s, focusing on artifacts like [[Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|Triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors]] and iron weapons{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=81-82}}{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=95-96}}.
Key contributions include the "layered model of prestige goods" proposed by [[Takehiko Matsuki]], which explored the distribution of iron arrowheads{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=83}}. However, the term was often overused without sufficient theoretical analysis, leading to the labeling of various items as prestige goods across different historical periods{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=83-86}}.
== Issues in Prestige Goods Research == [[Hitoshi Shitagaki]] has identified limitations in prestige goods theory, including its reliance on material remains and the neglect of non-material exchanges, such as labor and kinship dynamics{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=86-91}}.
== References == {{References help}} === Sources === * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|author=Junichiro Tsujita|title=The Ancient History of Mirrors|year=2019|publisher=[[KADOKAWA]]|isbn=978-4-04-703663-5}} * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|author=Hitoshi Shitagaki|title=State Formation in the Kofun Period|year=2018|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]]|isbn=978-4-642-09352-1}} == See Also == * [[Non-market economy]] * [[Theory of bronze mirror molds]]
