<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:威信財]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:威信財]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:威信財]]
'''Ishinzai''' (威信財, {{lang-en|prestige goods}}) refers to valuables that symbolize power, a term used in {{ill|Cultural anthropology|en|Cultural anthropology|WD=Q28598|lt=Cultural anthropology|1=Cultural anthropology}}{{sfn|Kotobank: Ishinzai}}. However, the definition of this term is not fixed. In Japan, it is often cited using K. Ekholm's definition as "products that are not necessary for material survival but are essential for maintaining social relationships." It is also frequently used in the sense of "valuable items that indicate prestige ({{lang-en|status symbols}})"{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=90}}{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=255-258}}.
Originally, the term was used in {{ill|economic anthropology|en|economic anthropology|WD=Q27841|lt=economic anthropology|1=Economic anthropology}} to refer to artifacts that played a role in chiefdom societies with advanced social stratification. These artifacts confirmed the status of the upper class through gifts and exchanges and established reciprocal relationships. The social system maintained by such artifacts is referred to as the "prestige goods system" and was proposed as a model to explain complex hierarchical societies prior to the formation of early states{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=255-258}}{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=258-259}}. However, in Japanese {{ill|Archaeology|en|Archaeology|WD=Q23498|lt=Archaeology|1=Archaeology}}, there has been misuse of the term by simply labeling specific artifacts as prestige goods without delving into their social functions or distribution patterns{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=81-86}}.
== Research in Western Studies == === Early Development of Prestige Goods Theory === The origins of prestige goods theory can be traced to the 1922 study of [[kula (exchange)|kula]] by {{ill|Bronisław Malinowski|en|Bronisław Malinowski|WD=Q184992|lt=Bronisław Malinowski|1=Bronisław Malinowski}} and the 1897 study of {{ill|potlatch|en|potlatch|WD=Q756880|lt=potlatch|1=Potlatch}} by {{ill|Franz Boas|en|Franz Boas|WD=Q76857|lt=Franz Boas|1=Franz Boas}}. These discoveries advanced the exploration of non-market exchange systems. In 1954, {{ill|Marcel Mauss|en|Marcel Mauss|WD=Q295393|lt=Marcel Mauss|1=Marcel Mauss}} highlighted in his work ''{{ill|The Gift|en|The Gift|WD=Q357153|lt=The Gift|1=The Gift}}'' the concept of "spiritual power" in gift exchange, showing that such exchanges were "total social phenomena" extending beyond economic aspects{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}.
Subsequently, studies of prestige goods in the West were divided into two approaches: those focusing on artifact exchange and those building frameworks for social structure and state formation theories{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=96-97}}. The latter approach was significantly influenced by {{ill|Karl Polanyi|en|Karl Polanyi|WD=Q318029|lt=Karl Polanyi|1=Karl Polanyi}}, who, in 1957, proposed the coexistence of two fiscal systems in ancient societies before monetary economies: "prestige good finance" using treasures like prestige goods as payment and "subsistence finance" using food{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}.
This perspective was adopted in the 1960s by {{ill|neoevolutionism|en|neoevolutionism|WD=Q1973379|lt=neoevolutionary anthropology|1=Neoevolutionism}} and {{ill|structural Marxism|en|structural Marxism|WD=Q2096707|lt=structural Marxism|1=Structural Marxism}}, leading researchers like Ekholm to emphasize how the circulation of goods and control over marriage maintained, reproduced, and developed social relationships{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}.
=== Prestige Goods System === [[File:Prestige goods system-en.svg|400px|thumb|Model of the Prestige Goods System]] Building on these findings, in 1977, {{ill|Jonathan Friedman|en|Jonathan Friedman|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jonathan Friedman|qq=|WD=Q3183368|1=Jonathan Friedman}} and Michael Rowlands proposed a five-stage process to describe the evolution of chiefdom societies into early states{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=255-258}}: * '''Tribal Systems''' – Societies based on unilineal descent groups ({{ill|Lineage|en|Lineage|WD=Q436660|lt=Lineage|1=Lineage}}) or clans that share common ancestry and engage in marriage and exchange. * '''Asiatic States''' – Societies where kinship groups are Ranked, and social positions are determined by marriage ties to higher-Ranked kinship groups. * '''Prestige Goods Systems''' – Chiefdom societies develop across regions, with exchanges and gifts of prestige goods between upper classes forming alliances. * '''Territorial/City-States''' – Economically centralized societies around specific centers or independently developed economies (city-states). * '''Empires''' – Societies unified politically and militarily.
This model demonstrated how prestige goods facilitated alliances between regions and maintained hierarchical relationships through gifts from upper to lower classes and tribute from lower to upper classes{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}. However, it also highlighted the fragility of such societies, as disruptions in the supply of prestige goods could lead to their collapse{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=258-259}}.
== Research in Japan == In Japan, theories of state formation were predominantly based on historical texts like the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' until the 1980s. However, with the decline of documentary sources and the advancement of source criticism, research shifted towards {{ill|Archaeology|en|Archaeology|WD=Q23498|lt=Archaeology|1=Archaeology}} and {{ill|Cultural anthropology|en|Cultural anthropology|WD=Q28598|lt=Cultural anthropology|1=Cultural anthropology}}{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=4-7}}{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=8-17}}.
=== Development of Prestige Goods Studies === Prestige goods theories were first introduced to Japanese archaeology in 1985 by Hazumi Anazawa. However, its full adoption began in the 1990s, focusing on artifacts like [[Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|Triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors]] and iron weapons{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=81-82}}{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=95-96}}.
Key contributions include the "layered model of prestige goods" proposed by {{ill|Takehiko Matsugi|ja|松木武彦|WD=Q11530773|1=Takehiko Matsugi}}, which explored the distribution of iron arrowheads{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=83}}. However, the term was often overused without sufficient theoretical analysis, leading to the labeling of various items as prestige goods across different historical periods{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=83-86}}.
== Issues in Prestige Goods Research == [[Hitoshi Shitagaki]] has identified limitations in prestige goods theory, including its reliance on material remains and the neglect of non-material exchanges, such as labor and kinship dynamics{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=86-91}}.
== References == {{References help}} === Sources === * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|author=Junichiro Tsujita|title=The Ancient History of Mirrors|year=2019|publisher=[[KADOKAWA]]|isbn=978-4-04-703663-5}} * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|author=Hitoshi Shitagaki|title=State Formation in the Kofun Period|year=2018|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]]|isbn=978-4-642-09352-1}} == See Also == * {{ill|Non-market economics|WD=Q111182436|1=Non-market economics}} * {{ill|Theory of bronze mirror molds|ja|同笵鏡論・伝世鏡論}} ==References== {{translated page|ja|威信財|version=102441388|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:威信財]]

However, the term was often overused without sufficient theoretical analysis, leading to the labeling of various items as prestige goods across different historical periods{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=83-86}}.
== Issues in Prestige Goods Research == [[Hitoshi Shitagaki]] has identified limitations in prestige goods theory, including its reliance on material remains and the neglect of non-material exchanges, such as labor and kinship dynamics{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=86-91}}.
== References == {{References help}} === Sources === * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|author=Junichiro Tsujita|title=The Ancient History of Mirrors|year=2019|publisher=[[KADOKAWA]]|isbn=978-4-04-703663-5}} * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|author=Hitoshi Shitagaki|title=State Formation in the Kofun Period|year=2018|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]]|isbn=978-4-642-09352-1}} == See Also == * {{ill|Non-market economics|WD=Q111182436|1=Non-market economics}} * {{ill|Theory of bronze mirror molds|ja|同笵鏡論・伝世鏡論}} ==References== {{translated page|ja|威信財|version=102441388|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:威信財]]
<!--Prestige goods--><!--Prestige goods--><!--Prestige goods--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q109598912}} [[Category:Ancient Japan]] [[Category:Archaeology of Japan]] [[Category:Cultural anthropology]] [[Category:Economic anthropology]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

revision.comment: Bot: strip HTML comments; consolidate 4 interlanguage link(s) into wikidata link template [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/25185113590|time triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
'''Ishinzai''' (威信財, {{lang-en|prestige goods}}) refers to valuables that symbolize power, a term used in {{ill|Cultural anthropology|en|Cultural anthropology|WD=Q28598|lt=Cultural anthropology|1=Cultural anthropology}}{{sfn|Kotobank: Ishinzai}}. However, the definition of this term is not fixed. In Japan, it is often cited using K. Ekholm's definition as "products that are not necessary for material survival but are essential for maintaining social relationships." It is also frequently used in the sense of "valuable items that indicate prestige ({{lang-en|status symbols}})"{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=90}}{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=255-258}}.
Originally, the term was used in {{ill|economic anthropology|en|economic anthropology|WD=Q27841|lt=economic anthropology|1=Economic anthropology}} to refer to artifacts that played a role in chiefdom societies with advanced social stratification. These artifacts confirmed the status of the upper class through gifts and exchanges and established reciprocal relationships. The social system maintained by such artifacts is referred to as the "prestige goods system" and was proposed as a model to explain complex hierarchical societies prior to the formation of early states{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=255-258}}{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=258-259}}. However, in Japanese {{ill|Archaeology|en|Archaeology|WD=Q23498|lt=Archaeology|1=Archaeology}}, there has been misuse of the term by simply labeling specific artifacts as prestige goods without delving into their social functions or distribution patterns{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=81-86}}.
== Research in Western Studies == === Early Development of Prestige Goods Theory === The origins of prestige goods theory can be traced to the 1922 study of [[kula (exchange)|kula]] by {{ill|Bronisław Malinowski|en|Bronisław Malinowski|WD=Q184992|lt=Bronisław Malinowski|1=Bronisław Malinowski}} and the 1897 study of {{ill|potlatch|en|potlatch|WD=Q756880|lt=potlatch|1=Potlatch}} by {{ill|Franz Boas|en|Franz Boas|WD=Q76857|lt=Franz Boas|1=Franz Boas}}. These discoveries advanced the exploration of non-market exchange systems. In 1954, {{ill|Marcel Mauss|en|Marcel Mauss|WD=Q295393|lt=Marcel Mauss|1=Marcel Mauss}} highlighted in his work ''{{ill|The Gift|en|The Gift|WD=Q357153|lt=The Gift|1=The Gift}}'' the concept of "spiritual power" in gift exchange, showing that such exchanges were "total social phenomena" extending beyond economic aspects{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}.
Subsequently, studies of prestige goods in the West were divided into two approaches: those focusing on artifact exchange and those building frameworks for social structure and state formation theories{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=96-97}}. The latter approach was significantly influenced by {{ill|Karl Polanyi|en|Karl Polanyi|WD=Q318029|lt=Karl Polanyi|1=Karl Polanyi}}, who, in 1957, proposed the coexistence of two fiscal systems in ancient societies before monetary economies: "prestige good finance" using treasures like prestige goods as payment and "subsistence finance" using food{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}.
This perspective was adopted in the 1960s by {{ill|neoevolutionism|en|neoevolutionism|WD=Q1973379|lt=neoevolutionary anthropology|1=Neoevolutionism}} and {{ill|structural Marxism|en|structural Marxism|WD=Q2096707|lt=structural Marxism|1=Structural Marxism}}, leading researchers like Ekholm to emphasize how the circulation of goods and control over marriage maintained, reproduced, and developed social relationships{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}.
=== Prestige Goods System === [[File:Prestige goods system-en.svg|400px|thumb|Model of the Prestige Goods System]] Building on these findings, in 1977, {{ill|Jonathan Friedman|en|Jonathan Friedman|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jonathan Friedman|qq=|WD=Q3183368|1=Jonathan Friedman}} and Michael Rowlands proposed a five-stage process to describe the evolution of chiefdom societies into early states{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=255-258}}: * '''Tribal Systems''' – Societies based on unilineal descent groups ({{ill|Lineage|en|Lineage|WD=Q436660|lt=Lineage|1=Lineage}}) or clans that share common ancestry and engage in marriage and exchange. * '''Asiatic States''' – Societies where kinship groups are Ranked, and social positions are determined by marriage ties to higher-Ranked kinship groups. * '''Prestige Goods Systems''' – Chiefdom societies develop across regions, with exchanges and gifts of prestige goods between upper classes forming alliances. * '''Territorial/City-States''' – Economically centralized societies around specific centers or independently developed economies (city-states). * '''Empires''' – Societies unified politically and militarily.
This model demonstrated how prestige goods facilitated alliances between regions and maintained hierarchical relationships through gifts from upper to lower classes and tribute from lower to upper classes{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=75-81}}. However, it also highlighted the fragility of such societies, as disruptions in the supply of prestige goods could lead to their collapse{{sfn|Junichiro Tsujita|2019|p=258-259}}.
== Research in Japan == In Japan, theories of state formation were predominantly based on historical texts like the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' until the 1980s. However, with the decline of documentary sources and the advancement of source criticism, research shifted towards {{ill|Archaeology|en|Archaeology|WD=Q23498|lt=Archaeology|1=Archaeology}} and {{ill|Cultural anthropology|en|Cultural anthropology|WD=Q28598|lt=Cultural anthropology|1=Cultural anthropology}}{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=4-7}}{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=8-17}}.
=== Development of Prestige Goods Studies === Prestige goods theories were first introduced to Japanese archaeology in 1985 by Hazumi Anazawa. However, its full adoption began in the 1990s, focusing on artifacts like [[Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|Triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors]] and iron weapons{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=81-82}}{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=95-96}}.
Key contributions include the "layered model of prestige goods" proposed by {{ill|Takehiko Matsugi|ja|松木武彦|WD=Q11530773|1=Takehiko Matsugi}}, which explored the distribution of iron arrowheads{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=83}}. However, the term was often overused without sufficient theoretical analysis, leading to the labeling of various items as prestige goods across different historical periods{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=83-86}}.
== Issues in Prestige Goods Research == [[Hitoshi Shitagaki]] has identified limitations in prestige goods theory, including its reliance on material remains and the neglect of non-material exchanges, such as labor and kinship dynamics{{sfn|Hitoshi Shitagaki|2018|p=86-91}}.
